name: Saliencia Lakes Loop – Somiedo Natural Park
name_original: Circular de los lagos de Saliencia — Parque natural de Somiedo
description:
If we think about Asturias and its lakes, for most people the first ones that come to mind are the mythical lakes of Covadonga. But those of Saliencia, in the Somiedo natural park, are a diamond that has nothing to envy.

On this Route you will discover the four lakes that make up this group: Calabazosa, Cerveriz, Almagrera lagoon and La Cueva. The path gives you endless panoramic views that will keep your mouth open throughout the entire excursion. It is a long route, with some fairly steep climbs, and is well marked with markings and milestones. Keep in mind that you will not find fountains, so it is essential that you bring enough water. 

The Route can be done all year round, but in winter you may find snow and in summer it is very hot and there is no shade at all. Finally, the starting point is located at the top of Farrapona. There is a parking lot here where you can leave your vehicle.
region_intro: Hiking around San Emiliano offers diverse and contrasting natural landscapes, characterized by abrupt mountain reliefs and deep valleys. The region features clear signs of glacial erosion and distinctive Karstic relief, contributing to its geological interest. Dense forests of beeches, oaks, and Pyrenean oaks cover areas like the San Emiliano Canyon, while the impressive Peña Ubiña, soaring to 2,417 meters, dominates the skyline. The area also encompasses parts of the Babia and Luna Valleys, providing varied terrain for exploration. Best hiking trails around San Emiliano The most popular hiking route is Saliencia Lakes Loop – Somiedo Natural Park, a 4.8 miles (7.7 km) trail that takes 2 hours 32 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores the scenic Saliencia Lakes within Somiedo Natural Park. Another top favourite among local hikers is Cresta Sierra de los Bígaros y Lago de la Cueva por la Farrapona — circular desde Torrestío, a difficult 12.6 miles (20.3 km) path. This challenging trail offers expansive ridge views and leads to the Cueva Lake. Local hikers also love the Pico el Comal desde el Puerto de Ventana — Parque Natural de Las Ubiñas-La Mesa, a 5.3 miles (8.5 km) trail leading through the Parque Natural de Las Ubiñas-La Mesa, often completed in about 2 hours 26 minutes. Hiking around San Emiliano is defined by abrupt mountain reliefs, deep valleys, and dense woodlands. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops around lakes to challenging ascents to peaks. The routes in San Emiliano are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from more than 160 reviews. More than 1500 hikers have used komoot to explore San Emiliano's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in San Emiliano?
A: San Emiliano offers a wide variety of hiking experiences with over 35 routes. These range from easy strolls to challenging mountain ascents, catering to different fitness levels and preferences.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes in San Emiliano?
A: The region is characterized by diverse and contrasting natural landscapes. You'll find abrupt mountain reliefs, deep valleys, and dense forests of beeches, oaks, and Pyrenean oaks, particularly in areas like the San Emiliano Canyon. The terrain also shows clear signs of glacial erosion and distinctive Karstic relief, especially around the impressive Peña Ubiña.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in San Emiliano?
A: Yes, San Emiliano features several circular routes. A notable challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1232210001/cresta-sierra-de-los-bigaros-y-lago-de-la-cueva-por-la-farrapona-circular-desde-torrestio" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cresta Sierra de los Bígaros y Lago de la Cueva por la Farrapona — circular desde Torrestío&lt;/a&gt;, which offers expansive ridge views and leads to Cueva Lake. For a moderate experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1605098990/saliencia-lakes-loop-somiedo-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saliencia Lakes Loop – Somiedo Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some natural landmarks or viewpoints to look out for?
A: The region is rich in natural features. You can explore the picturesque Somiedo lakes and the Barrios de Luna Dam within the Babia valley. The impressive Peña Ubiña, soaring to 2,417 meters, dominates the skyline and offers stunning views. Additionally, the San Emiliano Canyon is densely forested with ancient trees, providing a unique natural experience.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in San Emiliano?
A: While many routes involve varied terrain, there are options suitable for families. The region has 2 easy routes and 24 moderate routes. For a moderate, shorter option, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1605098990/saliencia-lakes-loop-somiedo-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saliencia Lakes Loop – Somiedo Natural Park&lt;/a&gt; is a good choice, taking around 2.5 hours to complete.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in San Emiliano?
A: The komoot community highly rates the trails in San Emiliano, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 160 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, from abrupt mountain reliefs to deep valleys and dense woodlands, and appreciate the variety of options available for different ability levels.

Q: Can I find any huts or shelters along the hiking trails?
A: Yes, there are several huts and shelters in the area. Notable options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3728513" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Meicín Mountain Hut&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6136136" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Teito of Braña La Corba&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6332943" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa Mieres&lt;/a&gt;. These can provide a resting point or shelter during your hike.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural attractions near the hiking routes?
A: Beyond nature, the region offers cultural attractions. You can find the Renaissance Quiñones palace, the Clerk's House, and walled enclosures in Riolago municipality. There are also emblazoned houses and vestiges of Iberian-Roman towns in surrounding villages, and a Roman Road indicates historical pathways that can be explored.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in San Emiliano?
A: San Emiliano's diverse landscapes make it suitable for hiking across multiple seasons. The fertile soil supports a variety of flora, including birches, which would be beautiful in spring and autumn. Given the abrupt mountain reliefs, summer offers more stable weather for higher altitude trails, while spring and autumn provide pleasant temperatures for lower valley walks.

Q: Are there opportunities for wildlife observation while hiking?
A: Yes, the abundant woods and diverse habitats in San Emiliano provide excellent opportunities for wildlife observation. You might spot birds of prey, chamois, roe deer, deer, wild boars, and otters. The region is part of the San Emiliano Valley Nature Park, which is dedicated to preserving this rich biodiversity.

Q: What is the longest challenging hike available in San Emiliano?
A: For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1232210001/cresta-sierra-de-los-bigaros-y-lago-de-la-cueva-por-la-farrapona-circular-desde-torrestio" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cresta Sierra de los Bígaros y Lago de la Cueva por la Farrapona — circular desde Torrestío&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 20.3 km (12.6 miles) path. This route typically takes around 7 hours 30 minutes to complete and offers extensive ridge views.

Q: Can I combine hiking with exploring mountain passes?
A: Absolutely. San Emiliano is home to several significant mountain passes. You can incorporate areas like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6333018" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Cubilla Pass&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/176266" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ventana Pass&lt;/a&gt; into your hiking plans. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1238650075/pico-el-comal-desde-el-puerto-de-ventana-parque-natural-de-las-ubinas-la-mesa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico el Comal desde el Puerto de Ventana&lt;/a&gt; route starts from a pass and leads through the Parque Natural de Las Ubiñas-La Mesa.
tips:
One of the lakes composing 'Lagos de Saliencia' - It is part of a Natural Park and overnight stays are not allowed. There are guards driving around and using binoculars.

It's a lovely route though and very worth cycling by! Tough loose gravel through, so suspension and thick tires are recommended.

Also known as the Mina lagoon, this small lagoon is formed between the Cueva lake and the Cerveriz lake. 

Depending on the time of year you may see it without water, since being very shallow and located in a limestone basin, it tends to dry out in the summers.

This lake, like the surrounding ones, is part of the group of Saliencia lakes.

You will be able to see that the water of this glacial lake is somewhat less transparent than the others. It usually has a dark green color.

Also known as Black Lake, you are facing the deepest lake in Asturias, as it can reach up to 9 meters deep. 

In the same valley of Lake Cerveriz, it is the basin of natural origin with the highest flow in this region.

waypoints.name: View of Lago de la Cueva
